%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how complex numbers are represented graphically, how to move between rectangular and polar forms, and how basic algebraic operations work on the complex plane.

Section B.1-2 introduces the **algebra of complex numbers** — the rules for adding, multiplying, and converting complex numbers. A complex number \(z = a + jb\) can be plotted as a point \((a, b)\) on the complex plane, giving every abstract number a concrete geometric home. From there, you learn a second, equally powerful description: **polar form** \(z = re^{j\theta}\), where \(r\) is the distance from the origin and \(\theta\) is the angle from the positive real axis. Mastering the conversion between these two forms — and knowing which form to use when — is the central skill of this section and appears repeatedly on exams.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.2](/figures/page-005-fig__b_2-1.png)
*Fig. B.2 shows \(z = a + jb\) as a point on the complex plane, making the rectangular coordinates \((a, b)\), the magnitude \(r\), the angle \(\theta\), and the conjugate \(z^* = a - jb\) all visible at once — this single diagram anchors every algebraic operation in the section.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The two faces of a complex number: rectangular form \(z = a + jb\) and polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) are the same point described two ways — the conversion formulas and the quadrant-check trap are shown in one reading path.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215327721-4926.png)

## Core Idea

Every complex number has **two equivalent descriptions** that you must be able to move between fluently.

**Rectangular form:** \(z = a + jb\), where \(a = \operatorname{Re}(z)\) is the real part and \(b = \operatorname{Im}(z)\) is the imaginary part. Note carefully: the imaginary part is the real number \(b\), not the term \(jb\).

**Polar (exponential) form:** \(z = re^{j\theta}\), where

$$r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}, \qquad \theta = \angle z = \arctan\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

The conversion is geometric: \(r\) is the straight-line distance from the origin to the point \((a, b)\), and \(\theta\) is the angle that line makes with the positive real axis.

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

The formula \(\theta = \arctan(b/a)\) gives only the **reference angle**. You must check which quadrant \((a, b)\) lies in and adjust \(\theta\) accordingly — a step students routinely skip.

**Quick example:** For \(z = -2 + j2\), \(r = \sqrt{4+4} = 2\sqrt{2}\) and \(\theta = \pi - \pi/4 = 3\pi/4\) (second quadrant, not \(\arctan(-1) = -\pi/4\)).

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A complex number \(z = a + jb\) is a point \((a, b)\) on the complex plane with real part \(a\) and imaginary part \(b\).
- Polar form \(z = re^{j\theta}\) uses magnitude \(r = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) and angle \(\theta\) — same point, different description.
- Always check the quadrant when computing \(\theta\): \(\arctan(b/a)\) alone is not enough.

*In the next section we will apply these two forms to Euler's formula and explore how multiplication of complex numbers corresponds to rotation and scaling on the complex plane.*
